The Tree Gods Are a Bit Testy
nytimes_articles.abstract:
In New Guinea, where many natives believe that certain forests are inhabited  by the spirits of ancestors, those who dare to cut sacred groves risk retribution in the form of shriveled crops, barren wives and other ills. 

     Roughly analogous woes recently descended upon Dr. Paul Dantzig, a dermatologist who apparently violated a local taboo when he cut down 95 trees on his own property in Greenwich, Conn. His medical practice dried up; his house was vandalized. He was, in effect, driven out of town.

WEDDINGS;Amanda Marsted, Jose Cruz Osorio
nytimes_articles.abstract: Amanda Gilbert Marsted, a daughter of Marcia Reid Marsted and Jeffrey G. Marsted of Canton, Conn., was married yesterday in Colebrook, Conn., to Jose Manuel Cruz Osorio, a son of Fidelfa Osorio de Cruz and Ismael Cruz Montes of St. Helena, Calif. The Rev. Ronald Newlin performed the ceremony at the Colebrook Congregational Church.         Ms. Marsted, 27, is keeping her name. She is a research analyst at New Frontier Capital, an investment partnership in New York. A cum laude graduate of Georgetown University, she received a master's degree in international affairs from Columbia University. Her father is a principal in Bradley, Foster & Sargent, an investment firm in Hartford. Her mother is a professional photographer and a research assistant at the University of Connecticut Health Center in Farmington.

From Parents' Worst Tragedy, A Living Memorial in Israel
nytimes_articles.abstract:
I'M very blessed," is not a phrase people expect to hear from a mother who watched her first child die at the age of 4 in her husband's arms less than a year after the diagnosis of a brain tumor. 

     But the mother, Lynn Gettenberg, often exceeds expectations. In the aftermath of the death of their daughter, Rachel, Mrs. Gettenberg and her husband, Chaim,  have raised several hundred thousand dollars by holding fund-raising parties for their neighbors once a year in a tent in their backyard in Lawrence.

DANCE;Nureyev in Mind, the Paris Opera Ballet Moves On
nytimes_articles.abstract:
WHEN THE PARIS OPERA Ballet moves into the Metropolitan Opera House tomorrow, it will be sailing under its own proud flag, a company at the peak of its powers. But it will also still be flying the pennant of Rudolf Nureyev, whose career in the West began here when he bolted to freedom at Le Bourget Airport in 1961 and ended with six years as the company's director and two as principal choreographer. 

     In choosing the repertory for this tour, Hugues Gall, the Paris Opera's director, and Brigitte Lefevre, its director of dance, wanted to pay homage to Nureyev with "La Bayadere," the last of his re-creations for Paris of the great  Russian romantic ballets of Marius Petipa. When the curtain rose on the sumptuous production at the Palais Garnier on Oct. 8, 1992, Nureyev was already  seriously ill, his death only three months away.

Programs to Deal With Fallout From AIDS
nytimes_articles.abstract:
IN the face of the spread of AIDS on Long Island, service providers are beginning to respond with programs intended to help mothers, children and adolescents to cope with the illness and its repercussions. 

     The supervisor of the preventive services program and chairwoman of the AIDS  Advisory Committee at St. Mary's Children and Family Services in Garden City, Barbara Schork, said St. Mary's also offered counseling for mothers and children, Meals on Wheels and services like taking children to visit parents in  hospitals. Ms. Schork said the agency, which has helped arrange guardianships for 100 children in five years, conducted follow-up sessions with the children to make sure that they were faring well.

What It Takes To Skate: Drive And Discipline
nytimes_articles.abstract:
VICTORIA OULLETTE, who is 12 years old and lives in Simsbury, has a passion for ice skating and hopes to compete at the U.S.A. National Championships within two years. 

     Victoria, who is called Tory, has been competing since she was 7 and trains at the International Skating Center of Connecticut in Simsbury. Her coach, Galina Zmievskaya, has trained 10 world and Olympic champions, including the 1994 Olympic gold medalist Oksana Baiul. Tory's ballet training takes place with Nina Petrenko, (above left), a choreographer for U.S.A. National, world and Olympic competitions. Tory's program is demanding: two to three hours daily, five days a week, on the ice, and then four to five hours a week in ballet and specialized gymnastics for skaters. Before and after school, she is living and breathing skating. As competitions approach, training time increases.

nytimes_articles.abstract: ACCORDION CRIMES By E. Annie Proulx. 381 pp. New York: Scribner. $25.    WORDS are chewy things for E. Annie Proulx. She loves to roll them around in  her mouth and bite down on them, the tougher the better. In "Heart Songs and Other Stories" (1988) and her first novel, "Postcards" (1992), she feasted on the jawbreaking language of northern New England, where the names of plants and  trees -- "miterwort," "tamarack" -- seem to mimic the hard soil they grow from.  In her second novel, "The Shipping News" (1993), which won both a Pulitzer Prize and a National Book Award, she moved farther north and east, to Newfoundland. There, on "the most utterly desolate and miserable coast in the world," she quarried even stonier words: "sunkers," "stribbled," "streeling," "skreel."

Prague's Historic Cemeteries
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THE Czech novelist and essayist Milan Kundera, writing about the character Sabrina, an exile from Bohemia, mentions that "the only word that evoked in her  a sweet, nostalgic memory of her homeland was the word 'cemetery.' " This odd statement gained new meaning for me when I visited some of the cemeteries of Prague. Notable for their beauty and tranquillity, burial grounds like the Slavin cemetery at Vysehrad and the Old Jewish Cemetery in Josefov also reminded me that the culture of a country and its people reveals itself through  its dead as well as its living. 

     My favorite view of the Old Jewish Cemetery is the one from No. 17 on Listopadu Street -- the busy thoroughfare that follows the banks of the Vltava.  With the clatter of traffic behind you, you can stand next to the silent cemetery wall and peek through the wooden door at the graves within. Like the cemetery itself, and perhaps like the city that contains it, this view is intimate, surprising: the sight of the crowded gravestones in the sun and shade.

nytimes_articles.abstract:
Even a bird enthusiast like Peter Mott was surprised to see the colony of parrots perched on the towering lampposts around the athletic field of Brooklyn  College. 

     "Do you hear them?" asked Mr. Mott, the president of the New York City Audubon Society, as he approached the field for his first look at the flock. "See, there goes one. That's their nest," he said, clutching a set of binoculars hanging from his neck. "It's paradise for parrots!"

FILM VIEW;Immigrants' Tales, in Subtle Shades of Gray
nytimes_articles.abstract:
SAM DEEDS, THE CRUSADING sheriff of Rio County, Texas, in the film "Lone Star," has crossed the border to investigate the death years ago of Eladio Cruz, a Mexican-American who used to smuggle friends into the United States by hiding  them among the watermelons in his pickup truck. 

     In a sleazy town just inside Mexico, Sheriff Deeds encounters one Senor Montoya, "the tire king," a grizzly old cynic who lived north of the Rio Grande  himself for 15 years. As they talk in the yard of his tire warehouse, Montoya takes a Coke bottle and draws a line in the gravel. He challenges Deeds to step  across the line.

nytimes_articles.headline: WEDDINGS;Elisa Shevlin, William Rizzo
BEST SELLERS: June 23, 1996
LIVES;Fear of Flying
nytimes_articles.abstract:
When I was 5 years old, a tornado lifted my family from our suburban neighborhood, in our Ford station wagon, and turned us onto our collective back  like a bug. Then, strangely enough, the tornado lifted our car once more, and righted us. Flatter, yes, but restored to our wheels, bodies more or less intact. Inside the car were: my father, my mother -- eight months pregnant with what would soon be my sister -- two of my brothers, a family friend and I.  My brother David and I were in what we called the "way back," the two facing seats in the rear of the car that folded down to create cargo space, up to accommodate children. 

     It was 1965: we lived in Wichita, Kan. We'd been out to dinner, a rare occasion in my family, when the storm began. Candles were lighted at the Ramada  Inn when the lights went out; I was allowed a Shirley Temple, also rare. The mood was festive. There was neither a tornado watch nor warning in effect. As we drove home, lightning struck, one thick bolt still imprinted in my memory, sizzling across a field.

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: BOERUM HILL/BROOKLYN HEIGHTS;Loud Church Is Irking Souls
nytimes_articles.abstract:
On a warm day last summer, neighbors of an evangelical church were fed up with the amplified organ, the loud preaching and shouts from parishioners wafting through the building's open doors. Several grabbed garden hoses, aimed them over a cinder block wall that divides the backyards of their brownstones from the church, and sprayed water into the sanctuary. Things didn't quiet down  until the police came. 

     Almost a year later, the pounding sounds coming from the national headquarters of the Universal Church of the Kingdom of God on Fourth Avenue have been muffled, but not enough to satisfy the residents of Bergen and Dean Streets.

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: BOERUM HILL/BROOKLYN HEIGHTS;The St. George Hotel: Back From the Brink?
nytimes_articles.abstract:
It took a long time for the St. George Hotel to devolve from a vacation spot  for some of the city's most dapper into a boarding house for some of the city's  most desperate. Still, George Scott plans to resurrect a bit of the hotel's former style by the end of the summer. 

     Mr. Scott, the executive director of Educational Housing Services, a nonprofit group, signed a four-year agreement with the owners of the St. George  on June 14 to transform the 111-year-old hotel, at 100 Henry Street, near Clark  Street, into student housing. The owners, Moshe and Shalom Drizen, will continue to manage the building, and began renovating the 12-floor structure last week.

FRESH AIR FUND;Mother Eases Her Worry With a Visit to a Camp
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The sky was brilliant blue and the sunshine warm, but Marina Shevchenko did not look like someone enjoying a weekend in the country.  She, her husband, Victor, and their 8-year old daughter, Sabena, stood at the edge of a lake recently catching fish with a bamboo pole as cicadas kept up a hum that some of  the city visitors nearby thought was heavy machinery. 

     Despite the delights of the day, Mrs. Shevchenko could not help worrying.

nytimes_articles.headline: Garden City, Now the Owner, Weighing Fate of St. Paul's Site
nytimes_articles.abstract:
AS an esthetic focal point around which the village has flourished for over a century, St. Paul's School has been regarded by Garden City residents as an integral part of their historic heritage. But now, the huge, Victorian Gothic structure has become a focal point of discussion and challenge about what to do  with it. 

     The school, sitting on 48.6 acres of prime real estate in the center of the village, has been empty for five years since the Long Island Episcopal Diocese,  citing financial losses, closed it in 1991. In 1993, villagers voted overwhelmingly to acquire the property through condemnation.

nytimes_articles.headline: We Knew What Glory Was
nytimes_articles.abstract:
When I was growing up in the 40's and 50's, my father would pack up the car every August and squeeze in my mother, four children, several dolls and a picnic lunch. It was the time before air-conditioning, and the drive was hot, dusty and, after New York, without bathrooms. 

     We left long before dawn, because for a dark-skinned man driving a large shiny sedan holding a white- looking wife, the journey from Connecticut to the South was not without peril. It was essential that each leg of the trip be made  before nightfall. We knew that safety lay within the homes and the churches of my father's friends and colleagues, the black ministers we would visit. They were our underground railroad.

SOAPBOX;Time of Your Life, Huh, Kid?
nytimes_articles.abstract:
HIGH school is a battleground for the heart. 

     The hallways and cafeterias and classrooms brim with teen-age rebellion, anxiety and sheer frustration. We're torn between childhood and adulthood. Although we must accept responsibility for our actions, we still have to ask our parents for lunch money and permission to stay out after curfew. Teachers demand that we keep up our G.P.A.'s and score high on our S.A.T.'s. Literature, television and films bombard us with images of the impossible, the ideal teen-ager.

THE SONG IS ENDED
A Massachusetts Farmer-Developer Sows Housing
nytimes_articles.abstract:
FORTY years ago, says John Magill, he "fell into" the housing development business in this historic New England town 40 miles southwest of Boston after rebuilding the barns destroyed by fire on his family's dairy farm. 

     Hundreds of homes later, the farmer-turned-developer has set Grafton's close-knit neighborhoods of colonial homes abuzz with a plan to turn 350 acres of his family's 500-acre farm into a $20 million golf course and housing development. Despite some controversy over its traffic and environmental impact, his plan is expected to go forward.

VOICES: VIEWPOINT;Filling a Void in Accounting
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THE battle over who chooses the people who set accounting standards seems likely to end in some sort of compromise, with Arthur Levitt Jr., the chairman of the Securities and Exchange Commission, prevailing in his effort to have people who represent the "public interest" added to the Financial Accounting Foundation. 

     The foundation oversees the Financial Accounting Standards Board, the quasi-official group that sets the accounting rules for America's publicly owned companies. Mr. Levitt says the foundation should be changed so that a majority of its members are people "with strong public service backgrounds, who are able  to represent the public interest, free of conflict."
